_meta.issue: #112-#118 [M15] Formative path S4 — Holak Scale (training module)
_meta.interaction: Growth-step classification (kind: classify) — formative exercise, NOT scored toward completion
_meta.source: Holak Scale — two critical maturity thresholds: 8→9 and 10→11. Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL examples SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). Formative module — no pass threshold, no certificate.

intro: The hardest part is not the first levels but two thresholds: 8→9 (from individual good practice to wiring AI into a process) and 10→11 (from one-off improvements to continuous improvement). This module shows what really separates these levels and how to plan the crossing. It is a growth exercise, not a test.

blocks.text: Many people and teams stop just before level 9 or 11. The 8→9 threshold is when one person's good practice becomes part of a process: a clear assistant/decision-maker split, reversibility and an audit trail. The 10→11 threshold is when improvements turn into a routine of continuous improvement and calibration.

items: 8→9: individual practice → a process with control and a trail.
10→11: one-off improvements → a measured, continuously calibrated system.
At both thresholds the key is not a new tool but a new working habit.

blocks.title: Holak Scale
blocks.text: The 8→9 and 10→11 thresholds are the most common places AI adoption gets stuck. More: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.

The most dangerous one is the „we are mature already” trap. Treating maturity as a finished state stalls growth just before 11. Real level 11 is not a goal achieved but a routine of measuring, calibrating and improving your own AI use.

blocks.title: A stall signal
blocks.text: If you use AI smoothly but do not measure its impact and do not calibrate how you judge output — you are probably at 10, not 11.
Next you will assign concrete steps to the right threshold: 8→9 or 10→11. No pass threshold — the goal is to recognize which step is your nearest crossing today.

interaction.title: Step 8→9 or 10→11?
interaction.intro: Assign each step to the threshold it belongs to. Feedback explains why it belongs to that crossing.

categories.label: Step 8→9
categories.desc: From individual good practice to a process with control and a trail.

categories.label: Step 10→11
categories.desc: From one-off improvements to measured, continuous improvement.

items.text: You wire AI into a process with a clear split: assistant proposes, a human decides.
items.rationale: Role split and wiring into a process is the essence of the 8→9 crossing.

items.text: For AI-supported decisions you ensure reversibility and an audit trail.
items.rationale: Control and a trail are the condition for reaching level 9.

items.text: You start sharing practices and calibrating output judgment together in the team.
items.rationale: Moving from individual to team practice is the 8→9 move.

items.text: You make auditing and calibrating AI use a fixed part of the team's routine.
items.rationale: Continuous calibration is the essence of level 11.

items.text: You measure AI's real impact on quality and time, and adjust your approach accordingly.
items.rationale: Measurement and correction are the condition for the 10→11 crossing.

items.text: You treat AI adoption as continuous improvement built into the strategy.
items.rationale: Continuous improvement instead of a one-off initiative is level 11.

points: Threshold 8→9: turn individual practice into a process with role split, reversibility and a trail.
Threshold 10→11: turn improvements into a routine of measurement, calibration and continuous improvement.
At both thresholds the key is a new working habit, not a new tool.
checklist: Do I know which threshold I am standing at today?
Is my nearest step concrete and reversible?
Do I measure AI's impact, or only use it?
summary.nextStep: Next step: pick one threshold (8→9 or 10→11) and plan one small, reversible experiment to climb higher.
